Hi, and welcome back to the celebrity branding blueprint. My name is Greg Rowlett. Once again, I am joined by Emmy Award-winning director, best-selling author, Mr. Nick Nanton. And here in this next video, we're talking about the second phase here in building your celebrity brand. And it's all about building your brand, right? So at this point, everyone should kind of understand some of the key fundamentals and foundations of the celebrity brand. And hopefully, they've gone through the exercises to develop their niche, right? And now that we have our niche, we need to develop the brand for that niche. What do we want people to know that we do, right? And I think everything for us revolves around stories. And I think this is a great place to kind of kick this off. In the intro, we talked about, really, your brand is really just your story, right? So let's talk about why stories are so important, some of the effects that stories have on the brain, and how does this all play a role in developing your brand? Sure. So one of the cool things, we just did a lot of research on this, obviously, for the storytelling. And the really cool thing, just the very simple thing to note, is that, number one, when someone hears a story, there's a chemical release in their brain called oxytocin. Oxytocin is literally kind of like this love chemical, and it builds trust. So one of the things to understand is that when you tell a good story, there's chemical emitted in someone's brain that actually builds trust. Now, the other thing about it is that most people would think, there's a left side of the brain, the right side of the brain. The left side is very kind of formulaic and methodical. The right side is very creative. Most people would think that stories appeal to the right side of the brain, the creative side. But what they like to learn is the fact that actually it appeals to the left side of the brain, because a story is just what helps people make sense of facts. So it really appeals to the factual, the methodical side of the brain. And again, it emits this chemical that helps build trust. So stories really work for you to help you do things that are hard to do with just facts or even features and benefits. The story can be remembered. It can build a relationship. It can build trust. Yeah, and I think that's really important. What you just said is that the stories help people make sense of things. Because when we say we get a brochure from a dentist or a financial advisor, who doesn't matter who it is, when we start just seeing bullet point after bullet point of feature, feature, feature, this, I don't remember any of that. But if we have a five minute conversation and you tell me about a story, that's what I remember and take with me. So let's talk about where to insert this story as we're introducing people to our brand. Sure. I mean, I think you should introduce, look, you can have multiple stories. Obviously, it depends on your marketplace. But it really all starts with your core story, I call it. It's all about who you are and where you came from. Because people want to know that. Whether we didn't start this video series with it, but I'll kind of tell you my shortened core story now, because it really makes sense. So I'm usually at some event where people have never heard of me before. I mean, because I've been brought into some new marketplace. And they maybe built me up a little bit. The more I can get my story told before I get there, we always try to do that. Pre-mailers, hotel room drops, anything that we can start telling our story ahead of time. That's huge. That's really helpful. So, but they don't really know who I am. And I don't really have any trust with them. So I typically start out. I'll tell you my brief story to you guys. So I'm from the island of Barbados. My family's been there for 300 years. We came in the 1600s as Welsh pirates. True story, right? So the kind of funny thing is as I was growing up, my dad kept asking me what I wanted to be. And the older I got, the funnier I thought it was to say I want to be a pirate. And the older I got, the less funny he thought it was. Until finally one day my dad came to me and said, hey, Nick, I know what you're going to be. And I said, what's that, dad? He says you're going to be a lawyer. That way you can still pillage. That's right. There you go. That's my only bad lawyer joke I usually tell. But it disarms people, it disarms people, gets involved with you, right? And it's true that I'm from Barbados. Then I go into the fact that, look, and the reason why I tell them and tell the story is, look, I'm here to talk to you about branding. Well, why is a lawyer here to talk to you about branding? Let me tell you a little bit of my story to make it all make sense. That's what they want to do. I want to make it make sense. And if you don't put them in a place where they understand the relevance of it before you start, you might as well not start. So I talk about the fact that I started playing guitar at 6, started songwriting at 16. I put out my first record at 18, of which I have about 800 copies still in my parents' bed. And I talk about the fact that it's because I didn't know the things I'm going to teach you today. But they're still there thankfully. And I want them to stay there forever. Because I hate that record now. Because we all grow and all those things. And everyone can laugh. They've all had that sort of scenario. They now see that I wasn't just born and everything went amazing. I still have had some struggle, right? And then at 18, I went out to the University of Florida. I usually get a couple of go-getters in the crowd, which is great. And I got the opportunity to work on everything from large-scale events to small events and music videos and stuff with Bill Cosby and President Bush and Don Shula and Bobby Knight and the cast of The Real World, and Roland Christopher Reeve before he passed away. And I say, I finished undergrad in about 2 and 1 half years. And I went to law school. Why did I go to law school? Well, for two reasons. Number one, I always wanted to be the president of a record label. And I knew a lot of them were lawyers and presidents of many major corporations were lawyers. And number two, my brother went to med school, so I had to do something. I certainly wasn't going to get into med school. People kind of laugh again. And so I talk about that. And I talk about the fact that when I went to law school, I literally sat down with the people next to me. And I said, they kind of put us in little groups by our last name, typical stuff. Hey, what kind of law are you interested in? Tell a little bit about yourself. And I was like, well, I'm not really interested in any law. I don't plan on practicing a day of law in my entire life. I just want to be the president of a record label. So if I have to pick, I'm going to learn some entertainment law stuff. Little did I know they don't have any entertainment law classes in my life. But I don't plan on practicing a day of law in my entire life. And they all were really, my group was really glad to hear this, because you know that whole look to your left, look to your right, one of these people is not going to be here, and it might be you. Well, they all thought it was going to be me. So the good news is I didn't fail out of law school. I finished law school, got out of law school, and actually had my first son a week after I took the bar exam. So that's a great timing. Right. So now I've got people kind of understanding, kind of feeling the tension with me on the story. And I say, look, so I went to my partner, Jack, who was then my mentor for many years. And Jack and I started talking about what to do. And he said, Nick, well, here's the deal. Look, you have a pretty unique skill set. You have, whether you realize or not, you've learned how to do celebrity buzz building and PR stuff and working with all these celebrities that you have. And you've learned how to do media creation, everything from posters to music videos to, I used to produce records, still do one every now and then. But sorry. But creating media that was interesting when I look back on it, when I was in college and undergrad law school, everyone I was working with, we practically had no budget. I mean, if we brought in a Bill Cosby, all the money went to pay Bill Cosby or whatever it was. Or if I worked with a band, they didn't have any money. So I had to learn how to create things that looked or sounded like Hollywood and Madison Avenue or whatever it is on a very small budget. So I learned how to create media that sold in its own way. And Jack said to me, he said, Nick, you know what, you may not realize about me. This is him talking now, that he had a skill set, a distinct skill set that he'd used to lead the sales and marketing efforts. So over a billion dollars worth of products and services, that's a billion with a B. That's a lot. That's a lot of stuff. Yeah, right? It's a billion with a B. Through his main two skill sets, which were personal branding and direct marketing, he said, Nick, you have a knack for these two things. And I understand some of about your two things. But if we combine all four of these things together, I think we can actually build you as one of the top entertainment lawyers in the country. And you can take care of your family while we figure out what else you want to do. So I said, OK, well, that sounds OK to me. So after I took the bar exam and passed the bar exam, within a year of practicing law full-time, I was billing it over three times the rate of really anyone I knew in New York, LA, we're in Orlando. Not that the place matters that much anymore, but certainly the big entertainment towns or the big buildings. Right, we're not the mecca of the music industry. LA and New York. I was billing it typically three times the rate of the guys who I knew were there practicing 30 years. And I did what any smart kid would do in their late 20s making about $1,000 an hour billing that out. And I said, hey, Jack, this has been great, but I don't want to do this anymore. And he kind of laughed. And he said, well, Nick, what do you want to do? And I said, well, I've realized there's people who travel around the country, travel around the world, and they teach people how to do things. And I think we can change people's lives. We've just figured out a formula on how to do this. And so if we go around and we teach people this formula, I think I can change a lot of people's lives. And also, I don't have to practice law. He said, well, not a bad idea. So we set out on that road. But what he said, the next thing he said to me is, Nick, I think it's a great idea, but you want to make the switch house sooner. Like, well, I got another baby on the way so soon. Like now, yeah. And he said, all right, well, here's what you need to do. The biggest problem you have is you're only testimonial. And many of us have had these things happen in our life. We figure something out that works for us, but we haven't tested anybody else. And he said, so I'm going to tell you what you need to do. I'm going to make this short and simple. You need to write a book. And similar to my newsletter discussion we talked about earlier, I said, well, like an e-book, that's cool. Everyone has e-books. And he said, no, no, no, a real physical hard copy book. I said, well, OK. He said, look, anytime I have everyone to build my expertise in any marketplace and any industry, I wrote a book on the subject. And then literally, I was the guy who wrote the book on it. So I said, OK, I'll bite. He had written, I don't know, 12, 15, 20 books before this. So I knew he knew what he was talking about. So we kind of sat down and we brainstormed how we would teach what we're going to teach, how to break it down into these five modules. And we came up with the topic for the book called Celebrity Branding You. And the cool thing about it was that from the time, this is a great secret. This is a writer downer as they come to us. From the time, Jack and I put the title to our book, Celebrity Branding You, and I said that I was the author of the forthcoming book, Celebrity Branding You. I got a huge bump of credibility. So if people didn't know when the book was coming out, people didn't ask who was publishing the book. They didn't even know if the book was coming out. They just knew that I was the author of this forthcoming book. I was an author. OK, so all of you watching today, figure out what the name of the book is you want to write. You got to start writing it. It can be a page a day. Whatever. And eventually, if you drag it out too long, people will start calling you on it. So you got to do it. But so put a title to your book, and you're going to get instant credibility by adding that to your bio. You're the author of this forthcoming book. OK, so Jack and I wrote the book in about six months, which is, I didn't know how to write a book, but as you can tell by my mannerisms, I'm not the kind of guy who likes to sit down to write for hours. So I kind of did drive by writing, a paragraph here, a paragraph there. We finished the book. And then I said to Jack, OK, cool. So we got the book now. I assume we can go to your publisher because he had just finished a three-book deal on a major publisher. I said, Jack, I assume we can just go to your publisher and get them to publish the book. He goes, well, make this not really how it works. My publishing deal is over. I've never written a book on this topic. So what we'll have to do now is we'll have to go find an agent who believes in the topic. The agent will have to find a publisher who believes in the topic. The publisher, we're going to have to try to negotiate with them or multiple publishers to essentially give away all of our rights for little to no money. First, some value distribution, but little to no money. And then once we figure that deal out, it's probably going to be another year from then before the book actually comes out because they got to schedule it into their schedule. So my jaw is kind of on the floor. And he said, I think you need a book like yesterday, don't you? I'm like, yes, yesterday. So I said, OK, what do we do? He said, well, let's self-publish. So without self-publish, it took me another 12 months, way too long to get the book out after that. But there's one thing I didn't realize. I went to all the guys who were designing CD covers and albums for me, and posters, and music stuff, and cool stuff. And thankfully, Jack had worked with books before a lot because I would have no clue. And every time we got the design and the proofs back, something was wrong. Either the margins were too wide, the spacing between lines was too big, the cover was too short to cover the whole hardcover book, all that sort of stuff. And he also told me, look, a hardcover book is what you need because hardcover books give you the maximum amount of credibility. And what I understood with all these mistakes was the fact that, look, I would rather hold the book back. I didn't want to hold the book back, but I'd rather hold it back and make it look and feel great because people, if they see a book that you have, they assume it's your life's work kind of, right? So if it doesn't look and feel great, if it looks like garbage, they're gonna assume you have really no value for what you know. The packaging of what you know is oftentimes more important than what you actually know because if people don't even, they don't even read it, they're gonna see the cover, they're gonna see how thick it is, they're gonna feel how nice it is. So I knew that. So once we finally got the book ready to get in the marketplace, I went to this guy who, Jack's former publisher, publicist, PR firm, they'd all hire, they paid this guy, he's really like a mercenary. And all he does is make books bestsellers. And so I volunteered to help the guy because I kind of want to know what he was doing, trying to want to figure it out. I thought it might be a valuable skill at some point. Well, little did I know how valuable it might be. But so I did everything I thought that guy had done. He wasn't very forthcoming about what he'd done, but I kind of tried to figure it out. So I did everything I thought he had done. And on the day we launched, I emailed everybody, I practically had ever met and said, help me, you know, buy this book, help me make the book a bestseller. I will wash your dog, I will do whatever you want me to do to help me out. And I blasted that all out. And I actually remember the date. It was October 31st of 2008. My oldest son, now he was three years old at the time, and he was going to be a pirate for Halloween, jokes later. And so I filmed him saying, arm at his by my daddy's book. And we shipped that video out on Halloween day and gave all these bonuses out and all this stuff. And the book had seven bestseller lists that day. And my life has literally never been the same since. That's when I started traveling around to small groups, 2030, the biggest one, like I said, I think it's 40 people giving away my book for credibility. So they would hear me, these groups, anyone who has an existing group, they need content. Anyone has a radio show needs content, a TV show needs content. You've got to convince them, you've got content. If you've got a book, a bestselling book, you have valid content. So I'd convince them, hey, I'm going to come and speak. Here's what, they're great. Now they got content. And I'm going to give everyone in the room a book. So even better, everybody who comes gets a free book. They love this idea. Then I'm building celebrity status. People have me sign the book and all that sort of stuff. So we built a seven figure business on the back of that. Now, what happened then was we got clients who said, hey, Nick, could you do the same thing for me? Somewhere through the time, energy, effort, money required to put out a book that we knew would help them. Jack and I lost about everybody. They're like, oh, I don't really, okay, I don't want to. Just too much. So we came up with this idea to write a book called Big Ideas for Your Business. And that book was, we were going to take our top 20 clients, each have to write just one chapter in the book and they're going to write one thing about what they know that relates to a big idea. Now what I told them all was, guys, I want you to show what I call a $10,000 secret. What's a $10,000 secret? It's the secrets you hold back for your top consulting clients. It's your best stuff that either your clients would pay you a lot of money and they come in for consulting days, whatever. And they all kind of let me perplex, Nick, why would I give away my best secrets? Well, there's two reasons for that. Number one, if you give away your best secrets for free or next to free in a book, people are going to say, man, that guy or that girl is so smart, imagine what I could get out of them if I paid them. Number one. Number two, I can give you a manual on how to build a jet engine. Does not mean you're going to want to build a jet engine. But if you needed a jet engine and the guy who wrote the book from Building Jet Engines was available to you and trying to convince you he was the right one, he's not going to have to convince you very much, right? So they all kind of got it. So I gave him some time, they all wrote the chapter, brought it back to me. I did everything I'd done, the editing, the design, the everything else, and I put the book out again. We launched it together. We made 20 more people best sellers that day. And now we've done that for over 1400 authors all around the world. That's a big piece of what we do. Now that's mainly where I stop my story and I move into, but now let's talk about how this all relates to you. I want to teach you some skills. Now there are pieces of my life that are certainly missing in that story. There's gaps of time I just gloss over. You know, one of the things you have to be careful about is I tell the story that's relevant each place I tell it. It might sound like I'm lying. If you heard the two stories, they're not. It's just I left that one part, I added another part based on what's relevant to the audience. And I'm very careful. I try not to give too many time frames and that sort of thing because it's easy to forget. There's time gaps and things. Yeah, it's just happened over the course of life. But what I love about that story is, you know, a lot of people familiar with comic books, right? Batman, Superman, that is your planet, Krypton blowing up, coming to earth, becoming a superhero, story, right? And all of a sudden I'm relevant to that audience and when I start talking about being a best-selling author or branding, like, well now they understand what this lawyer from Barbados guy is talking to me about being a best-selling author. All this stuff is lining up. And even more interestingly, they now have some level of trust with me because typically they've heard about my family, they've heard about my story. It wasn't just a rise to fame. No one gave me a million bucks and said, go do it. There are points that they can resonate with. And they think, okay, he did this and he's done this for other people. Maybe he is the guy who can help me, right? Exactly. So what I want you to do in your action guide is I want you to write down some of the hooks and things that you remember from that story because again, you're gonna remember pieces from that story better than if you just came up and say, hey, I'm gonna help you be a best-selling author. You're gonna get this, man, or any of our books. Now you do get into that, right? When you go into that part of it. But right now everyone knows that at six you started playing guitar, at 16 you started songwriting. I mean, those are the things that we all relate to because especially me, I played music. I was a musician at 16, I was cutting my first record. At 18 I was going on tour. So all of these different connection points are the ways that you relate to each other. So this origin story is super important. So what I want you to do right now is to write down some of the things that you remember from Nick's story because what we're gonna do next is help you to create your version of this story. So I mean, that's the million dollar question is now how do I create something similar, right? How do I create my Batman story? How do I create my Superman story for myself? Right, and so what you really gotta do is start, and we talked a little bit about this in the last module. You gotta start looking at those pivotal points in your life that led you to where you are today. I talked to clients and oftentimes, look, even there's painful parts of your life that you do not wanna share with people. Look at whether or not that may be relevant because every, if you imagine your life is like a hallway and someone's shining a really bright light down the end of the hallway, every door you close is dimming that light a little bit. And we all, not to be cornered, we all kinda have a light to share with the world, right? So the more you can utilize those pivotal points where those doors you might wanna shut them, there are things that wouldn't be relevant and you wouldn't wanna talk about. But we had a bankruptcy lawyer who was really scared to talk about the fact that he had filed bankruptcy. I'm like, that's like a golden ticket. But people who are really nervous to call you because they feel like they failed anyway, something got them in a place they didn't wanna be in. And they feel like someone's judging them, but you've been through it. Like this is great. Why didn't you tell me this six months ago, right? Yeah, we've been much further along. Right, and so the fact is you've gotta start looking at these pivotal points and start trying to figure out how to tell that story. I think if you, also if you ask other people, you should try it on other people, start telling them about things. See if you're engaging them, see what's keeping them interested. I always love to bring a little bit of levity of things. I love to make it a little bit funny, talk about things, make jokes, crack jokes at yourself. I think that's very healthy to do that people know you don't take yourself too seriously. But you've really gotta start looking at where am I today, what am I trying to convince people of? Where do I start? And now what are the important dots to connect in the middle? And some are for business reasons, some are for personal reasons, right? And you just gotta start connecting those dots. Yeah, so a really good exercise in your action guide is a timeline, and there's a timeline in there for you to fill out. And fill it out with the different relevant points. Maybe there's stuff from your youth. Maybe it was you came over to this country from somewhere else. Maybe you moved from the big city to the country, your country to big city, but start there, and then find all those pivotal points that help you to get your point across. Great exercise for you to do. And Nick, what I wanna talk about now is there's some people right now who are just like, you know what, I don't wanna tell people that all that personal stuff, when I had my first child, or failure points in their life, or some people are very private, right? And I think it's okay to wanna have those values, but what happens when you kinda get over that? When you get over that, you'll see that people will start to see you for who you really are. One of the reasons they can't see you is they don't know who you really are. So you'll start to see the way people react. And again, we talked briefly about it before Dan Candy calls the concept dog whistles. People who've been through certain things that you have been through, it's like a dog whistle. They hear it and they lie down to it. Everyone else just goes, okay, that's cool. Then they kinda move right past it. So the more you want to be vulnerable, the more people will also trust you because they'll believe that if you wanna share those things with them, you're not gonna be hiding other things that are of much less significance in daily life. Right, yeah, again, it goes back to the foundational principle, people buy people. They buy from people they know they can trust. If I don't know you, I don't trust you enough to hand you my credit card to do business with you, much less start drilling and filling in my mouth, or help me find my dream home, or whatever the case is. So you really need to get over that. And for those who maybe are a little more reserved, what are some examples of things that they could share? To at least, sometimes it's baby steps, right? And it doesn't start with telling that. The first time I told my story, it wasn't that elaborate. Now I kind of have that elaborate story. And when you tell it to a live audience, whether it's one person or a thousand people, you start to see what people are really like. I mean, you almost get some amens in there, right? It's like, oh yeah, and people laugh out loud. I mean, you really start to grab the audience and pull them together as a whole unit. And when you can create that, and you feel that energy with the audience that are moving with you, that's when you know that you're gonna be successful. But I mean, you can start with everything from talking about the fact that you have kids, or that you coach their baseball team, talking about the fact that you go to church, you can talk about the fact that it's a sports team you really like, and how they had a great season or a horrible season last year, or you can't wait until they get rid of that pitcher, or whatever it is. It could be just really surface things. But some of your favorite books, favorite TV shows, but again, it's points of affinity, things that people will go, one of the biggest objections people have to hiring you is, that guy can't understand me. He doesn't know what I've been through, he doesn't know where I've come from. On a very core level, it's like people who are involved in domestic abuse and things like that, they don't think anyone possibly can understand the situation they've been in. I'm not making a lot of that, because I've never been in that scenario, and it's a horrible thing, but you see people who have horrible traumatic things. One of the biggest problems is that they think that no one's ever been through this, and they know what other people have, but never like them, whether it's drug problems, alcohol problems, abuse, whatever it is, and once they realize that's what 12-step groups are for, they start sharing, they start realizing they're not alone in this. And when you encounter a customer or a client, a prospect, they have a problem, that's why they would hire you. Some problem, it may not be a serious problem, it might be that they wanna make more money, but if you can draw them along this journey with you, they see that you can understand them, you can understand their problem. Oh, well maybe this person, now that they understand me, maybe I trust me enough to take me on that journey. Yeah, so I think hopefully what you're understanding now is that your brand is your story. It really is, well, it's as simple as that, but it doesn't mean that creating your story is super simple. So it really goes back to creating your story, and then I think that the second integral part is you get to tell it, right? So you have to tell it using media, and I think that that's really big. So you just told your story in this video, potentially depending on how you're getting this course, maybe you saw it in a sales video, maybe they saw you on stage presenting your story. You tell your story in every tell a seminar that you do, every webinar that you do, you tell your story on CD, you tell your story in all the book chapters that you do. But I can't tell you my story, because I've heard it a million times. But it's funny, I always get worried because someone in the audience might have heard it once or twice, they still laugh. They weren't listening to Nick tell it every day for the last year, they weren't thinking of Nick ever, probably, and then hear it again, it's like the first time they ever heard it. So don't worry about that. And every time I don't tell my story, I lose money, because people don't understand where I'm coming from, like they do when I get a chance to tell my story. The other thing we have a formula in the book, it's called a 5WH. And if you can answer that formula, we'll put it in the workbook too, who, what, when, where, why, and how. It's a very simple- You guys have all heard that one before, right? If you can answer those questions of who am I trying to help? When do they need, when do they identify that they would need my help? Where would this go down? If you answer those questions, you'll start seeing, well, what can I relate in my story? What in my story will connect to these points to help these people understand each of these points of the story? Right, definitely. So again, that's a great exercise, the 5WH. It really, we've heard all the who, what, when, where, why, how, right? So, it's something that we were taught in like elementary school when we were talking about book report or something. But now you're just relating it to your life, right? And your story, and again, use each of those 5WHs in the H, and then find pieces and chunks of story that you can insert into each of them, and it will really definitely help you out. And so, what we need you to do right now is we need you to create this story, create this brain, because in the next step, now we're gonna show you how to take that story and help elevate you to celebrity expert status. Absolutely, I think the cool thing just to talk about real quick, there's a guy who does a talk called The Golden Circle, and this guy Simon Sinek, super cool, and I think a great place for anyone to start, even in these questions, is to start with Y. And when you start with what you're gonna do, some people start with what I'm gonna do. Well, I'm gonna go help people do X, and then they go to how am I gonna do it, well, I'm gonna do it this way. And they may never get to Y. The most successful people, the most successful brands in the world, start with a big Y. Why are they doing this? The reason why people resonate with my story is the fact that they see the Y I wanna do, I realized that I was doing something that made me money, but I didn't really wanna be doing it. Whether people have made money or they're not making money, there's oftentimes had been things that they didn't really wanna be doing. And the fact that I figured out this formula that worked for me, and I kind of proved for the story that worked for me, and now I can help other people have that same success, that's my Y, that's my driving passion, is to help other people, to help the most people, help the most people. When they understand the Y, everything else falls into place. So you may even start on the 5WH and the Y.
